title: Input and Output
description: Read user input and convert between types.
objectives: Convert strings to numbers with int() and float()
recap: input() reads text from the user (always as a string), and you can convert it with int() or float(). f-strings make formatting output easy.
practicePrompts: Ask the user for their birth year and print how old they are.
Can you format a price to always show exactly 2 decimal places?

practiceExercises.title: Double the number
practiceExercises.prompt: Read a number from input(), convert it to an int, and print `Double is X` where X is the number times two. Use the stdin tab to provide `7` for testing.
practiceExercises.goal: Use input() + int() + an f-string to double the input.
practiceExercises.starterCode: # Read a number, double it, print the result

practiceExercises.title: Rectangle area
practiceExercises.prompt: Read a width and height (two separate input() calls), convert both to float, and print `Area: <area>`. Pre-fill stdin with two lines like `3` and `4` (you should see `Area: 12.0`).
practiceExercises.goal: Read two numbers and print their product with a label.
practiceExercises.starterCode: # Read width, height, print area as a float

practiceExercises.title: Personal greeter
practiceExercises.prompt: Read a name and an age (two inputs), convert the age to int, and print `Hello <name>! You'll turn <age+1> next year.` Provide stdin lines like `Alex` and `17`.
practiceExercises.goal: Combine two inputs and a calculation into one f-string.
practiceExercises.starterCode: # Read name and age, greet them, show next year's age
hints: Two input() calls — one for each piece.
int() only on the age, not the name.
F-strings can do math inside braces: f"{age + 1}".
